title: Scaling WebSockets: Sticky Sessions, Fan-Out & Backpressure
description: Running real-time WebSockets across a fleet: why connections need sticky sessions, how a pub/sub backplane fans messages across nodes, tracking presence, per-connection backpressure with `bufferedAmount`, reconnect storms, and the file-descriptor ceiling. The fan-out math is verified in Node.

questions.title: You put 3 WebSocket servers behind a per-request round-robin load balancer, and connections keep breaking. Why — and what's the fix?
questions.description: Contrast a long-lived WebSocket with an ordinary stateless HTTP request.

options.text: A WebSocket is a long-lived, stateful connection pinned to one server, so the load balancer needs sticky sessions (connection affinity) to keep a client's traffic on the server holding its socket.
WebSockets require the balancer to re-distribute every message; enable per-message round-robin.
The servers are CPU-starved; adding cores will stop the disconnects.
Round-robin is fine; the real fix is turning off TLS.

questions.explanation:
The WebSocket upgrade establishes a single TCP connection that lives in **one** backend process. A balancer that routes per *request* can send the `Upgrade` to server A but later steer traffic toward B — which has no such connection — and the session breaks. The fix is affinity: balance at Layer 4 (per TCP connection, which naturally stays put) or pin with Layer-7 session stickiness.

Stateless HTTP avoids this entirely because any server can answer any request — which is exactly why a stateful, sticky connection is harder to operate at scale.

questions.title: Clients are spread across 3 WebSocket servers. A message from a user on server A must reach a recipient connected to server B. What architecture solves this?
questions.description: Server A's process holds only its own sockets — not B's.

options.text: A shared pub/sub backplane (e.g. Redis Pub/Sub, NATS): every server subscribes, and each forwards published messages to its own locally-connected clients.
Have server A open a fresh WebSocket to server B for every message it needs to relay.
Store the message in the recipient's localStorage until they happen to reconnect to server A.
Broadcast a UDP packet to the whole subnet and let the right server pick it up.

questions.explanation:
Each server's map of `userId → socket` is **per process**; server A simply has no handle to a socket living on B. A pub/sub bus decouples *publishing* a message from *which* server holds the recipient: every app server subscribes to the relevant channels, and when a message is published, each server delivers it to whichever matching clients it happens to hold.

This backplane is exactly what Socket.IO's Redis adapter, Phoenix PubSub, and similar tools provide — it's the standard way to scale WebSockets past a single box. Mesh-connecting the servers to each other instead — opening server-to-server sockets to relay messages — is O(n²) and brittle.

questions.title: Which read-only `WebSocket` property reports how many bytes you've queued with `send()` that haven't yet gone out to the network?
questions.description: It's your signal that the consumer is falling behind. Give the property name.

questions.title: Which statements about tracking presence (who's online) across a WebSocket fleet are correct?

options.text: Presence is distributed state: a user may be on any server, so you need shared storage or pub/sub to assemble the full picture.
If a server crashes, its clients' presence entries can go stale, so TTL/heartbeat expiry is needed to reap them.
One user can hold several simultaneous connections (tabs/devices), so presence is often ref-counted per user.
Because the connection is persistent, the server always receives a clean disconnect event, so presence is always exact.

questions.explanation: Presence is deceptively hard. It's **distributed** (a user's connection can be on any node, so no single process knows the global online set without shared state). Disconnects are frequently *not* clean — abnormal closures (code `1006`), network drops, and especially a crashed server fire **no** close event, leaving ghost entries. The cure is heartbeats plus TTL keys (e.g. a Redis key refreshed by each ping and auto-expired when pings stop). And since one human often has multiple tabs/devices, you typically **ref-count**: a user goes offline only when their last connection drops.

questions.title: You broadcast a fast stream, and one client is on a slow network. If you ignore its outbound buffer, what happens on the server?
questions.description: Where do the bytes a slow client hasn't received yet actually live?

options.text: That client's outbound buffer grows in the server's memory — a slow consumer can exhaust server RAM unless you bound the buffer (drop, coalesce, or disconnect it).
The slow client automatically throttles the broadcast for everyone else too.
TCP quietly drops the surplus messages, so server memory stays flat.
The event loop blocks until the slow client has caught up.

questions.explanation:
Bytes you've sent but the client hasn't acknowledged have to wait *somewhere* — the server's per-connection send buffer (`ws.bufferedAmount` on the server side). One slow client just delays itself, but thousands of slow clients can pile up enough buffered data to OOM the server.

The writes are non-blocking (the loop never stalls) and TCP won't drop your application data, so nothing saves you automatically. You must bound it: watch `bufferedAmount`, then drop/coalesce frames for laggards or disconnect them. This is the per-connection, fleet-scale version of Node stream backpressure.

questions.title: A WebSocket server holding 50,000 connections crashes, and every client tries to reconnect at once. What's the risk and the mitigation?
questions.description: Think about the load the surviving servers suddenly absorb.

options.text: A thundering-herd reconnect storm can overwhelm the surviving servers; clients should reconnect with randomized exponential backoff (jitter) to spread the load over time.
Nothing to worry about — the load balancer queues all 50,000 reconnects with no impact.
Clients should reconnect instantly and in lockstep to restore service as fast as possible.
No client may reconnect until the crashed server itself comes back online.

questions.explanation: 50,000 simultaneous reconnect attempts land as an instantaneous load spike on whatever capacity remains — which can tip the survivors over too, cascading the outage. The client-side cure is the same jittered exponential backoff used for ordinary reconnection (see the transports quiz), now mattering at fleet scale: spreading reconnects across a window keeps the surge bounded. Server-side, keep capacity headroom and rate-limit new connections. Reconnecting in lockstep (no jitter) is precisely what *causes* the herd.
questions.references: [Amazon Builders' Library: Timeouts, retries, and backoff with jitter](https://aws.amazon.com/builders-library/timeouts-retries-and-backoff-with-jitter/)

questions.title: Why is a stateless HTTP API generally easier to scale horizontally than a WebSocket service?
questions.description: Consider what each request/connection needs from the server that handles it.

options.text: Any HTTP request can be served by any instance (no per-connection state), whereas WebSockets pin long-lived stateful connections to specific servers, needing affinity plus a pub/sub backplane.
HTTP transfers fewer bytes per message than WebSockets do.
WebSockets cannot be load-balanced at all.
HTTP responses are always cached by the client, so the server does no work.

questions.explanation: Statelessness is the whole trick: because an HTTP request carries everything needed to serve it, you can add nodes and let any of them handle any request — horizontal scaling is almost free. A WebSocket inverts that: the connection is long-lived and bound to one process, so you must keep its traffic sticky **and** add shared state (a pub/sub bus) for any cross-node delivery. That statefulness — not bandwidth — is the core scaling tax. (WebSockets *do* have lower per-message overhead than repeated HTTP requests, but that helps throughput, not horizontal scaling.)

questions.title: You broadcast 5 messages per second to 100,000 connected clients. How many individual sends per second is that across the fleet?
questions.description: Each broadcast message must be delivered to every connected client. Give a single number.
questions.explanation:
Fan-out amplifies: one logical message becomes N physical sends, one per subscriber. So 5 messages/sec × 100,000 clients = **500,000** sends/sec.

```js
console.log(100_000 * 5); // 500000
```

The cost scales **linearly with connection count**, which is the hidden price of "just broadcast it." It's why broadcast-heavy systems lean on an efficient backplane, edge fan-out, or — when traffic is one-way — a simpler push transport like SSE. Doubling your users doubles your send rate at the same message frequency.

questions.title: Which are valid ways to keep a client's WebSocket traffic pinned to a single backend?
questions.description: Select every approach that actually works.

options.text: Layer-4 (TCP connection-level) load balancing, which naturally keeps a single connection on one backend.
Cookie-based or IP-hash session affinity at a Layer-7 load balancer.
Re-running the WebSocket upgrade handshake on every frame to re-pick a server.
Serializing the live socket into a shared database so any server can resume it.

questions.explanation:
Two real mechanisms: **L4 balancing** routes per TCP connection, so a WebSocket — being one long connection — stays on whichever backend accepted it; and **L7 affinity** (a sticky cookie or hashing the client IP) pins HTTP-level routing to one server.

The false options misunderstand what a socket is. The upgrade handshake happens **once**, not per frame. And a live socket is OS-level state — a file descriptor plus kernel TCP buffers bound to one machine — so it can't be serialized into a database and "resumed" elsewhere. Moving a connection means dropping it and reconnecting.

questions.title: What OS-level resource does each open WebSocket consume on the server, and why does it cap one machine's connection count?
questions.description: Think about what the kernel hands you for every accepted connection.

options.text: Each connection is a TCP socket = one file descriptor; the process/OS file-descriptor limit (plus per-connection memory) bounds how many a single server can hold.
Each connection spawns a dedicated OS thread, so the thread limit is the ceiling.
Each connection reserves a CPU core for its entire lifetime.
Connections are essentially free; a single server can hold an unlimited number.

questions.explanation: Every accepted socket is a **file descriptor**, and processes have an fd limit (`ulimit -n`) on top of kernel and application memory per connection. That's the ceiling for a single box — the classic **C10k problem** (and today C10M) is about pushing one server toward hundreds of thousands of concurrent connections using event-driven I/O (`epoll`/`kqueue`) instead of a thread-per-connection model. Modern servers handle many connections per *thread*, so threads/cores aren't the per-connection unit. Once you hit a box's practical limit, you shard connections across servers and glue them with a pub/sub backplane.

questions.title: For a live data feed (say, cursor positions in a collaborative app), how should you handle a client whose send buffer is backing up?
questions.description: Does every queued frame still matter once a newer one exists?

options.text: Drop or coalesce stale frames and send only the latest — for ephemeral real-time data, the newest value supersedes queued ones, so shedding old frames preserves freshness.
Always buffer every frame in order and never drop one, no matter how far behind the client gets.
Migrate that client to a server with a faster CPU.
Replay the entire message history to the client once its buffer finally clears.

questions.explanation:
Not all data needs lossless, ordered delivery. For ephemeral state — a cursor position, a live gauge, a presence ping — only the **latest** value matters; queuing stale intermediate frames just adds latency and grows memory. So when `bufferedAmount` climbs, coalesce to the newest value (keep one `pending` frame, overwrite it) or drop the backlog:

```js
let pending = null;                // newest wins
for (const v of [10, 11, 12, 13]) pending = v;
console.log(pending);              // 13 — send just this
```

Contrast this with chat or financial events, which need at-least-once delivery and ordering (see the delivery-guarantees quiz). The right move always depends on the cost of dropping a message — match the strategy to the data.
